rationale: The cited paper is used repeatedly to frame the core research gap and to position the new contribution, not just as a passing reference. The citing paper summarizes Yin et al. (2021)'s finding that slow motion with human actors increases intentionality inferences and can hurt product evaluations, then explicitly argues its own context (product-only videos without people) and mechanism differ. It presents its contribution as moving beyond that prior social-inference account to an immersion-based pathway for luxury perceptions, and also borrows procedural precedent (e.g., speed-manipulation conventions and some measures) from that work.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Yin et al. (2021) by examining a different ad context and outcome: from human-consumption ads and intentionality backfire to product-focused videos and perceived luxury. It introduces a distinct mechanism (immersion and expected hedonic value) while treating the cited findings as prior evidence in the same slow-motion marketing stream.
